schools.summary: Ellicott: only Pharez was literally Judah's son; the rest are tribal clans called 'sons of Judah,' and Carmi may be read as Chelubai.
Clarke explains the doubled genealogy as drawn from a second public register with remarkable particulars; Wesley notes the names show Shobal's descent from Judah.
Gill traces the posterity solely in the line of Pharez, the one strict son.
Matthew Henry: Ezra is most particular over Judah because that tribe, with Simeon, Benjamin, and Levi, made the surviving kingdom that returned from captivity.
Keil and Ellicott knit the Zorathite clans to Shobal (cf. 1 Chron. 2:52-53), reading the verse as a supplement to the earlier register; Ahumai and Lahad occur nowhere else.
Gill identifies Reaiah with the Haroeh named before, the two names of one signification.

Keil and Ellicott face the corrupt 'father of Etam': Keil supplies 'families,' Ellicott takes it as the dominant houses of Etam; both weigh the LXX/Vulgate 'sons of Etam' as a conjectured evasion, and read Hazelelponi as a sister-town.
Gill, with the Targum, takes it of the princes who dwelt in Etam, near Zorah.
Keil and Ellicott gather Penuel and Ezer as sons of Hur, firstborn of Ephratah and father of Bethlehem (1 Chron. 2:19,51), noting Gedor is claimed for both Penuel and Jered (v.18).
Gill reads 'father' as prince or founder and would identify Penuel with the earlier Hareph.
Keil and Delitzsch fix Ashur as Hezron's posthumous son (1 Chron. 2:24), number Naarah's four sons, and distinguish this Hepher from the Gileadite, refusing the unsupported land-of-Hepher conjecture.
Gill sets Ashur's pedigree as a son of Hezron by Abiah.
Keil and Delitzsch round off Naarah's branch, marking her four sons as met with only here and refusing to invent a history for them.
Gill notes only that of these men we have no account elsewhere.

themes: names kept though faces are forgotten

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch fix the Kethibh/Keri of Jezoar/Zohar, warning that a shared name (with Simeon's Zohar, Gen. 46:10; Abraham's Hittite, Gen. 23:8) is no proof of kinship.
Clarke would restore 'Coz' after Ethnan with the Targum, lest Coz stand in the next verse as a consequent without antecedent, and equates him with Kenaz.
Gill notes only that Helah was Ashur's other wife.

Keil calls vv.8-10 a fragment of unclear connection; Ellicott ties it to the Hurite scribes of Jabez (1 Chron. 2:55), reads Anub as 'grape-town' (cf. Matt. 7:16), and thinks 'and Coz' fell from the text.
Gill takes Coz simply as another son of Helah, reading the fragment as a continuation.
Clarke: Jabez ought to be named at the end of this verse, else he stands next as a consequent without an antecedent.
Matthew Henry makes the honour the heart of the verse — Jabez dignified by grace and providence — and grounds it in his learning (the scribes of 1 Chron. 2:55) and his piety as a praying man.

Ellicott glosses 'sorrow' as 'pain,' likens the naming to Rachel's Benoni (Gen. 35:18), and ties Jabez to the sons of Coz (v.8); Keil and Delitzsch record, only to reject, the guess that Jabez was Othniel (v.13).
Wesley reads the sorrowful name as a double memorandum — of the mother's thankfulness and of the vale of tears into which the child was born.
Gill weighs the traditions (wiser in the law, wealth, courage), settling on a man of devotion, and rejects the Jabez-Othniel identification.
Matthew Henry unfolds the four petitions — bless me indeed (Gen. 22:17), enlarge my coast, thy hand with me (Deut. 33:7), keep me from evil — as a pattern of prayer to the covenant God.

Ellicott reads the prayer as a suppressed vow, a condition like Jacob's at Bethel (Gen. 28:20), and finds in the double play on the name (cf. v.9) a mark of the section's antiquity; 'God granted' is literally 'God brought to pass.'

Wesley sets the prayer at some great and dangerous undertaking ('enlarge my coast' = drive out the Canaanites); Clarke makes it a meditation on earnest prayer for body and soul, dreading sin and suffering alike.
Gill notes the Syriac and Arabic read the prayer in the third person, as a blessing wished upon him, though the Hebrew keeps it his own cry.
Keil and Delitzsch make Chelub a form of Caleb/Chelubai, distinct from the two famous Calebs, and refuse to equate Shuah with Hushah, leaving the branch's attachment open (the men of Rechah).
Gill supposes Shuah the same as Hushah (v.4), so that Chelub descends from Ezer.

debates.question: Is Shuah the same man as the Hushah of v.4?

sides.position: yes — so Chelub is son of Ezer, descended from Hur
no — Shuah is otherwise unknown; the identification is unwarranted

schools.summary: Gill glosses Bethrapha as the house of Rapha.
Clarke relays the Targum's grand identification of 'the men of Rechah' with the great Sanhedrin; Wesley reads the name soberly as Rechab, a town not elsewhere mentioned.
Keil and Delitzsch derive Kenaz from Hezron (via Caleb the Kenizzite, Num. 32:12; Josh. 14:6) and read 'sons of Kenaz' as distant descendants, since Othniel and Caleb were brothers (Josh. 15:17).
Gill sets Kenaz as son of Chelub or Eshton, and had earlier kept Othniel distinct from Jabez.
Matthew Henry honours the valley of craftsmen — industrious tradesmen not to be reckoned mean men, who lived together to improve their arts.
Keil and Delitzsch set Meonothai, Ophrah, and Joab father of the valley of Charashim plainly in place.
Wesley reads Joab as father, that is chief or founder, of the inhabitants of the valley.
Gill notes Meonothai as another son of Othniel.

themes: skill of hand among God's people

schools.summary: Ellicott frames Caleb the Kenizzite (Josh. 14:6,14) grafted into Judah as the house of Caleb, and lays out the options for the broken clause 'the sons of Elah, even Kenaz.'
Clarke recalls Caleb's courageous piety and integrity (Num. 14:24) and the Targum's play on his name.
Gill insists Jephunneh is not Hezron, so this Caleb is the Kenizzite and brother of Othniel, the very spy.

Keil resists tying the clans to the towns of Ziph (Josh. 15:24), calling their connection lost; Ellicott reads town and clan together and hears in Ziph and Asareel an Edomite-Kenizzite stock absorbed into Judah.
Gill supposes Jehaleleel a son of the earlier Uknaz.

Keil and Ellicott declare the verse corrupt — a missing mother for 'and she bare,' two wives of Mered tangled together — and weigh the LXX's 'Jether bare Miriam' and the Syriac/Arabic omission as evidence of a faulty text.
Gill guesses Ezra a son of the Asareel just named.
Ellicott restores the displaced clause after v.17, splitting Mered's house into a Judahite and an Egyptian group, and reads Bithiah ('daughter of Iah') as a convert or an absorbed Egyptian clan.
Matthew Henry takes her as a literal Egyptian princess married into Judah before the bondage, while Joseph's merits were fresh.
Gill reads Jehudijah simply as the Jewish wife, set against the Egyptian.
Clarke relays the Targum's allegory turning every name into a title of Moses, making Bithiah his foster-mother whom 'Mered'/Caleb took.

debates.question: Who was Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh, and when did Mered's marriage fall?

sides.position: a literal Egyptian princess married into Judah before the oppression
a convert ('daughter of Iah') or an Egyptian clan absorbed into Mered's house

themes: a daughter of Pharaoh in Judah

schools.summary: Ellicott, taking the Hebrew 'sons of Hodiah's wife,' would emend to 'his Jewish wife, sister of Naham,' and sets Keilah (Josh. 15:44; 1 Sam. 23:1) and Eshtemoa in the Calebite division.
Gill reads Hodiah as another wife of Mered, but reports the reading that takes Hodiah as a man's name.
Keil reads Benhanan as one name and notes the list breaking off after Benzoheth; Ellicott, since the whole section traces the house of Caleb, infers these too are Calebite clans.
Gill supposes Shimon another son of Mered, or the same as Shammai.

themes: the sons of Shimon and Ishi

schools.summary: Matthew Henry makes the linen-weavers a matter of honour — the best weavers in the kingdom, raising their children to the same trade, not aiming to make them gentlemen.
Clarke passes on the Targum's gloss that they wrought fine flax for the garments of kings and priests; Wesley marks the turn to Shelah's line.
Gill explains the order: Pharez was traced first for the honour of David and Messiah; now the writer returns to Shelah.
Keil and Delitzsch note the dropped conjunction marking Shelah as a second co-ordinate line, and place Mareshah at the ruin Marash (Josh. 15:44).

Matthew Henry reads 'these are ancient things' as a meditation on providence — a family that once ruled in Moab, its heirs now brought low; those in dominion know not what their posterity may be reduced to.

Wesley takes the dominion as held for the kings of Judah and hears a sigh over blessed days now past in Chaldean servitude; Clarke relays the Targum's tie of Joash and Saraph to Mahlon and Chilion of Ruth.
Gill can only mark where the Vulgate parts from the resistant Hebrew.
Keil and Delitzsch puzzle over how far the description reaches, inclining to take it of all Shelah's sons but the linen-weavers.

themes: a proud past, a servile present

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'plants and hedges' as the towns Netaim and Gederah (Josh. 15:36), and 'the king' as a king of Judah whose royal potteries these were, like those of Sevres.
Wesley reads 'the king' as the king of Babylon and the potters as mean spirits preferring exile to the return; Clarke, dismissing the Targum, sees only brickmakers about the king's fences.
Gill reads it as a come-down — once-famous men now of mean employments.

debates.question: Who is 'the king' with whom the potters dwelt?

sides.position: a king of Judah, at a royal pottery in the land
the king of Babylon, in the drudgery of exile

themes: Netaim and Gederah or plants and hedges
the king of Judah or of Babylon

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott reconcile the lists — six sons in Genesis/Exodus, five here and in Num. 26:12 (Ohad dropped), Jarib for Jachin, Nemuel/Zerah for Jemuel/Zohar — and note Shaul the son of a Canaanitess (Gen. 46:10).
Clarke judges the divergent lists irreconcilable, occasioned by one man's several names — to attempt it useless.
Gill explains Simeon set before Reuben because its inheritance lay within Judah.
Matthew Henry frames the tribe's whole story: never of great note, increasing but not like Judah.

debates.question: Can the divergent lists of Simeon's sons be reconciled?

sides.position: yes — by copyist variants and alternate names
no — reconciliation is impossible and the attempt useless

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch fix Shallum as son of the Canaanitess's line and trace it through six generations to Shimei, noting the unexplained division of the list at 'sons of Mishma.'
Gill sets the plain succession — Shallum, Mibsam, Mishma.
Keil and Delitzsch bring the line to its head in Shimei of the sixteen sons, drawing the contrast with the brethren who had not many, so the tribe never matched Judah.
Gill names Hamuel, Zacchur, and Shimei.
Clarke sets the census numbers side by side (Simeon fallen to the smallest tribe); Wesley traces the failure to increase to Jacob's curse and Moses' passing Simeon by.
Ellicott reads 'his brethren' as the fellow-tribesmen and Simeon's absorption into Judah as the fulfilment of 'I will divide them in Jacob' (Gen. 49:7).
Gill notes Shimei's many children are nowhere named.
Matthew Henry draws the gentler lesson: those whom God increases should be thankful, even seeing others more increased.
Keil and Ellicott identify Simeon's eighteen cities within Judah's domain (Josh. 19:1), matching them to the parallel and marking the mistaken repetition of Sheba after Beersheba.
Gill notes the cities set down in nearly the same order as the older allotment.
Matthew Henry marks that the cities allotted to Simeon are here recorded as in Joshua's day.

themes: a distinct people on borrowed ground

schools.summary: Ellicott, with Rashi and Kimchi, reads the five villages as the remnant Simeon kept when the thirteen were lost, and weighs Etam against Joshua's Ether.
Gill, citing Kimchi, takes these five as all that remained by David's day, driving the tribe to seek new pasture.

themes: what was kept and what was lost

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott read the closing clause as Simeon's own registration — though its settlements lay within Judah, its clans were enrolled as a distinct tribe (Baal = Baalathbeer, Ramath-negeb, 1 Sam. 30:27).
Gill identifies Baal with Baalathbeer.

themes: a tribe without its own land

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch set the passage as the Hezekian expedition, the thirteen princes its leaders, their pedigrees untraceable to the earlier families.
Gill introduces them as men of rank, courage, and valour, unnoticed elsewhere, and notes Jamlech's likeness to Jamblichus.

themes: the muster of a bold venture

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch take the summary as the ground of the narrative: the fathers'-houses increased to a multitude, and so the princes went out for room and pasture.
Gill reads them plainly as the principal men, heads of their fathers' houses.

themes: increase as the spur to conquest

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch deny 'entrance of Gedor,' reading the phrase as 'the west of Gedor' (balancing the 'rising'/east), and rule out the Judahite Gedor (Josh. 15:58).
Matthew Henry reads it as a locality in Arabia, the entrance of Gedor, which the Simeonites seized.
Gill takes it as a place distinct from Judah's Gedor, perhaps the Gedaris of the Philistine borders.

debates.question: Does 'the entrance of Gedor' name a place or a direction?

sides.position: a place — the town the raiders reached
a compass-point — 'the west of Gedor,' the direction they traveled

schools.summary: Ellicott savours the wide, quiet land like Laish (Judg. 18:7), its dwellers Hamites (1 Chron. 1:8); Keil adds the Meunites among them, 'utterly destroyed' by the old word of the ban.
Clarke guesses the dwellers Philistines or Egyptians; Wesley draws the providence — God appoints the bounds of habitation, and the valley lay open to a people Israel had authority to expel.
Gill sets the discovery in or near the valley of Gedor.

Ellicott reads the victims as the Meunites of Maon and glosses the ban (devoting to God, cf. Josh. 6:17, with the Moabite Stone parallel); Keil marks the word's fading religious force into plain extermination, dated by 'unto this day.'
Gill identifies the actors as the princes written by name.

Keil traces the five hundred to the sons of Shimei and the 'escaped of Amalek' to the survivors of Saul and David's wars (1 Sam. 15:7); Ellicott ties the expedition closely in time to the raid on Gedor.
Gill sets the five hundred's march in the land of Edom.
Ellicott reads the 'remnant of the survivors' of Amalek, refugees in Seir from Saul and David (1 Sam. 15:7), a partly Edomite stock (1 Chron. 1:36).
Clarke, with Calmet, dates the expedition near the captivity of the ten tribes, when Simeon's remnant pressed south into Edom.
Gill recalls the swords of Saul and David the survivors had escaped.
Matthew Henry gathers the close: the curses on Ham and Amalek accomplished, and Simeon's curse of scattering (Gen. 49:7) turned to a blessing of preservation.

Keil weighs the conjectures over the count of five villages against Joshua's four; Ellicott sets Bilhah, Ezem, and Tolad among the towns of the extreme south of Judah (Josh. 15:26), their sites now unknown.

Keil and Delitzsch trace Jehu's three-generation descent and weigh the formula 'those who have come with their names' — the men expressly mentioned — distinguishing it from a similar phrase elsewhere.

Ellicott reads the town-names (house of chariots, village of horses, house of lionesses, two gates = Sharuhen) and asks whether 'unto David' means the cities passed from Simeon then, Ziklag being a Philistine borough.
Clarke has David seize the cities from a disloyal Simeon for Judah; Wesley lays the loss to Simeon's own sloth in never taking them from the Philistines, so that David annexed the forfeit.

debates.question: Why were these cities Simeon's only 'unto the reign of David'?

sides.position: David seized them from a disloyal tribe and annexed them to Judah
Simeon forfeited them by its own sloth in never taking them from the Philistines

schools.summary: Ellicott alone traces Bethuel (Chesil/Bethul), Hormah the ancient Zephath 'the ban' (Judg. 1:17), and Ziklag, David's town (Josh. 15:30; 1 Sam. 27:6), through their variant forms and later history.

themes: obscure towns of great later fame

schools.summary: Keil groups the seven names (with Ziza's five-generation pedigree) among the princes of multiplying houses; Ellicott reads Jaakobah as a patronymic 'to Jacob,' fastening the obscure prince back to the patriarch.

themes: bound to the stock of Israel
